title: Adverse Drug Reactions (ADR) وعوامل الاستجابة للدواء
summary: تتأثر الاستجابة للدواء بعوامل داخلية (العمر، الجينات، الأمراض) وخارجية (التحمل، التفاعلات الدوائية). التفاعلات الضارة للدواء (ADR) هي أي تأثير ضار غير مقصود يحدث عند جرعات علاجية عادية. تُصنَّف ADR إلى خمسة أنواع (ABCDE): المتوقعة (Type A)، وغير المتوقعة (Type B)، والمزمنة (Type C)، والمتأخرة (Type D)، وتلك الناجمة عن الإيقاف المفاجئ (Type E). يتطلب الوقاية منها اليقظة في الوصف والمراقبة.

key_drugs.name: Insulin
key_drugs.note: Type A ADR: نقص السكر (Hypoglycemia) - متوقع وذو صلة بالجرعة

key_drugs.name: Warfarin
key_drugs.note: Type A ADR: نزيف - متوقع، قابل للتدارك بتعديل الجرعة

key_drugs.name: Beta-blockers
key_drugs.note: Type A: انخفاض الضغط. Type E عند الإيقاف المفاجئ: نقص تروية عضلة القلب

key_drugs.name: Penicillin
key_drugs.note: Type B ADR: تفاعل تحسسي (Anaphylaxis) - نادر، غير متوقع، عالي الوفيات

key_drugs.name: NSAIDs
key_drugs.note: Type C ADR: قرحة هضمية مع الاستخدام المزمن

key_drugs.name: Corticosteroids
key_drugs.note: Type C: متلازمة كوشينج مع الاستخدام المطوّل. Type E عند الإيقاف: قصور الغدة الكظرية

key_drugs.name: Phenytoin
key_drugs.note: Type D ADR: Fetal Hydantoin Syndrome - تشوهات جنينية إذا استُخدم أثناء الحمل

key_drugs.name: Diethylstilbestrol
key_drugs.note: Type D: يزيد خطر سرطان المهبل في البنات المولودات لأمهات استخدمنه

mnemonics.title: تصنيف ADR: ABCDE
mnemonics.body:
A = Augmented (متوقعة، ذات صلة بالجرعة - الأكثر شيوعاً)
B = Bizarre (غريبة، غير متوقعة، مستقلة عن الجرعة - نادرة وقاتلة)
C = Chronic (مزمنة، مرتبطة بالاستخدام الطويل)
D = Delayed (متأخرة - تُكتشف بعد انتهاء العلاج)
E = End of treatment (الإيقاف المفاجئ للدواء)

mnemonics.title: Type A vs Type B: المتوقع والمفاجئ
mnemonics.body:
Type A كالمطر في الشتاء: متوقع، يمكن التحضير له بمظلة (تعديل الجرعة). شائع لكن نادراً يقتل.
Type B كصاعقة صيفية: مفاجئة، نادرة، لكنها تقتل (وفيات عالية). لا تتوقعها من الجرعة - هي طبيعة المريض. مثل: صاعقة Penicillin على مريض حساس.

mnemonics.title: الحوادث الضارة الخطيرة: DDHDC
mnemonics.body:
D = Death (وفاة)
D = Disability (عجز)
H = Hospitalization (استشفاء)
D = Dangerous/Life-threatening (مهدد للحياة)
C = Congenital Anomaly (تشوهات خلقية)

mnemonics.title: عوامل تغير استجابة الدواء: جسر
mnemonics.body:
تخيل جسراً يربط الدواء بالتأثير. على الجسر عوائق:
- طرفا الجسر (الأطفال والمسنون): كبد وكلى ضعيفة - الجسر أطول (t1/2 أطول)
- في المنتصف: الجينات (fast/slow acetylators) تحدد عرض الجسر
- تحت الجسر: الأمراض (كبد، كلى) تهز الأعمدة
كل هذه العوامل تُغيّر مآل الدواء وتأثيره.

mnemonics.title: Corticosteroids: سيف ذو حدين
mnemonics.body: كورتيزون: معجزة علاجية، لكن استخدامه الطويل يُشبه عقداً مع شيطان. في النهاية: متلازمة كوشينج (Type C). وإذا أوقفته فجأة: الغدة الكظرية نسيت تعمل وحدها - قصور كظري (Type E). الحل: التوقف التدريجي.

flashcards.front: What is the WHO definition of an ADR?
flashcards.back: Any harmful, unintended, and undesired effect occurring at doses used for treatment. It is not an overdose, and not side effects in the narrow sense.

flashcards.front: What is a Type A ADR? Give an example.
flashcards.back: Augmented: predictable, dose-related reactions resulting from the usual pharmacological effect of the drug. Common, with low mortality. Example: Insulin → hypoglycemia. Preventable by adjusting the dose.

flashcards.front: What is a Type B ADR? Give an example.
flashcards.back: Bizarre: unusual, unpredictable, dose-independent, rare but with high mortality. Example: Penicillin → anaphylactic shock (anaphylaxis). Treatment: stop the drug and avoid it in the future.

flashcards.front: What is a Type C ADR? Give an example.
flashcards.back: Chronic: resulting from prolonged chronic use, related to dose and time. Example: NSAIDs → peptic ulcer. Corticosteroids → Cushing syndrome. Treatment: reduce the dose or taper gradually.

flashcards.front: What is a Type D ADR? Give an example.
flashcards.back: Delayed: appears after treatment has ended, rare, and difficult to detect. Example: Phenytoin → Fetal Hydantoin Syndrome. Diethylstilbestrol → vaginal cancer in daughters of mothers who used it.

flashcards.front: What is a Type E ADR? Give an example.
flashcards.back: End of treatment: occurs upon abrupt withdrawal of the drug. Example: Beta-blockers → myocardial ischemia. Corticosteroids → adrenal insufficiency. Treatment: reintroduce the drug and taper gradually.

flashcards.front: What are the intrinsic factors that affect drug response?
flashcards.back:
1. Age
2. Genetics/Pharmacogenetics
3. Physiological state and diseases
4. Tolerance
5. Drug-Drug Interactions

flashcards.front: Why does the drug effect differ at the extremes of age (neonates and the elderly)?
flashcards.back: Both extremes have reduced elimination capacity: the liver and kidneys are less mature or less efficient. As a result: longer t1/2 and a stronger, more prolonged effect. Both require lower doses.

flashcards.front: What is pharmacogenetics?
flashcards.back: Variation in drug response due to genetic differences in metabolism. Example: Fast vs Slow Acetylators of Isoniazid (INH). This leads to differences in drug efficacy and the likelihood of side effects.

flashcards.front: How does severe liver cirrhosis affect a drug?
flashcards.back:
1. Low albumin → increased free fraction → adverse effects
2. Reduced hepatic blood flow → reduced drug elimination
3. Impaired microsomal enzymes → reduced metabolism → drug accumulation

flashcards.front: What is the FDA definition of a Serious Adverse Event?
flashcards.back: Any effect that causes: death, a life-threatening condition, hospitalization/prolongation of hospitalization, disability, congenital anomalies, or requires intervention to prevent permanent harm.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between idiosyncrasy and allergy in ADRs?
flashcards.back: Both are Type B. Allergy (hypersensitivity): an immunological reaction. Idiosyncrasy: an unpredictable reaction due to a genetic abnormality.

flashcards.front: What are the methods of ADR detection?
flashcards.back:
1. Subjective: the patient's complaint
2. Objective: direct observation of the event
3. Abnormal finding: abnormal results on clinical examination or laboratory testing

flashcards.front: What are the principles of ADR prevention?
flashcards.back:
1. Do not prescribe except for clear indications
2. Appropriate dose, route, and frequency
3. Ask about other medications
4. Ask about a history of previous ADRs
5. Inform the patient about potential serious effects
6. Appropriate laboratory monitoring

flashcards.front: What is the timing of onset of an acute ADR?
flashcards.back: Within 60 minutes of taking the drug.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between acute, sub-acute, and latent in ADR timing?
flashcards.back: Acute: within 60 minutes. Sub-acute: 1-24 hours. Latent: more than two days.

flashcards.front: Why are Type B ADRs considered the most dangerous despite their rarity?
flashcards.back: Because they have high mortality and are difficult to predict. They are not dose-related, so reducing the dose does not prevent them. The only prevention: stopping the drug and avoiding it in the future.

flashcards.front: What is an example of an idiosyncratic reaction in anesthesia?
flashcards.back: Some patients develop apnea (cessation of breathing) during anesthesia due to a genetic abnormality in the metabolism of the anesthetic drug.

flashcards.front: What are the intrinsic factors affecting drug response?
flashcards.back: Age, genetics (pharmacogenetics), disease state, tolerance, and drug interactions.

flashcards.front: Why are neonates and the elderly more sensitive to drugs?
flashcards.back: Because drug elimination is less efficient → a greater and more prolonged effect (↑ t½) → both require a lower dose.

flashcards.front: How does liver disease affect drug response?
flashcards.back: Severe cirrhosis → low albumin (↓ protein binding), ↓ blood flow and enzymes → ↓ clearance and metabolism → side effects.

flashcards.front: How does renal failure affect drugs?
flashcards.back: ↓ elimination of renally excreted drugs (such as Digoxin) → accumulation and toxicity.

flashcards.front: What is tolerance?
flashcards.back: A decrease in response to the usual dose over time, requiring an increase in the dose.

flashcards.front: What is the Rawlins–Thompson classification of ADRs?
flashcards.back: Type A (Augmented - predictable, dose-dependent) and Type B (Bizarre - unpredictable, dose-independent, usually immunological).

flashcards.front: Give an example of a Type A ADR
flashcards.back: Bleeding from Warfarin, hypoglycemia from Insulin - predictable and dose-dependent.

flashcards.front: Give an example of a Type B ADR
flashcards.back: Anaphylaxis from penicillin - unpredictable and dose-independent.

mcqs.q: What is the WHO definition of an ADR?
options: Any drug effect occurring at an overdose
Any harmful, unintended effect occurring at therapeutic doses
The expected side effects of any drug
mcqs.explanation: ADR per WHO: any harmful, unintended, and undesired effect occurring at doses used for treatment. It does not include overdoses.

mcqs.q: A patient taking Insulin develops hypoglycemia. This is an example of:
mcqs.explanation: Type A (Augmented): predictable, dose-related, resulting from the normal pharmacological effect. Insulin → hypoglycemia is typical of Type A. Preventable by adjusting the dose.

mcqs.q: A patient taking Penicillin develops anaphylactic shock (anaphylaxis). This is an example of:
options: Type A ADR - common and dose-related
Type B ADR - rare, unpredictable, and with high mortality
Type C ADR - from chronic use
Type E ADR - from drug withdrawal
mcqs.explanation: Type B (Bizarre): unpredictable, dose-independent, rare but with high mortality. Anaphylaxis with Penicillin is an immunological/allergic reaction typical of Type B.

mcqs.q: A patient taking Corticosteroids for a long time develops Cushing syndrome. This is an example of:
mcqs.explanation: Type C (Chronic): occurs with prolonged chronic use, related to dose and time. Corticosteroids → Cushing syndrome with long-term use = Type C.

mcqs.q: A patient abruptly stops Beta-blockers and develops myocardial ischemia. This is an example of:
mcqs.explanation: Type E (End of treatment): occurs upon abrupt withdrawal of the drug. Beta-blockers → myocardial ischemia upon abrupt withdrawal = Type E. Solution: reintroduce the drug then taper gradually.

mcqs.q: Fetal Hydantoin Syndrome caused by Phenytoin is an example of:
mcqs.explanation: Type D (Delayed): appears after treatment has ended, rare and difficult to detect. Fetal Hydantoin Syndrome = fetal malformations appearing after pregnancy = Type D.

mcqs.q: Which of the following factors makes both neonates and the elderly more sensitive to drugs?
options: Reduced drug elimination capacity → longer t1/2
Decreased tissue distribution of the drug
mcqs.explanation: At both extremes of age, hepatic and renal efficiency is lower, which increases t1/2, prolongs the drug effect, and increases the risk of toxicity. Both require lower doses.

mcqs.q: What is the main difference between allergy and idiosyncrasy in Type B ADR?
options: Allergy is more common than idiosyncrasy
Allergy is dose-related while idiosyncrasy is not
Allergy is an immunological reaction; idiosyncrasy is a genetic abnormality in metabolism
mcqs.explanation: Allergy (hypersensitivity) = an immunological reaction. Idiosyncrasy = an unpredictable reaction due to a genetic abnormality. Both are Type B: rare and dose-independent.

mcqs.q: حسب FDA، أي من التالي لا يُعدّ Serious Adverse Event؟
mcqs.explanation: الغثيان الخفيف ليس Serious Adverse Event. الحوادث الخطيرة: وفاة، تهديد للحياة، استشفاء، عجز، تشوهات خلقية، أو ما يستلزم تدخلاً لمنع ضرر دائم.

mcqs.q: أفضل إجراء وقائي لتجنب ADR هو:
options: وصف الدواء فقط بدواعٍ واضحة مع الجرعة المناسبة ومراعاة التاريخ الدوائي للمريض
تجنب سؤال المريض عن الأدوية الأخرى
mcqs.explanation: الوقاية تشمل: وصف بدواعٍ واضحة، جرعة مناسبة، السؤال عن الأدوية الأخرى وتاريخ ADR، إخبار المريض بالآثار الخطيرة، والمراقبة المخبرية.

mcqs.q: لماذا يحتاج كبار السن جرعات أقل؟
أي مما يلي Type B ADR؟
mcqs.explanation: التأق تفاعل مناعي غير متوقع لا يعتمد على الجرعة.
